name: Genome Nexus
description: description='The Genome Nexus project is designed to provide a comprehensive resource for the fast, automated, and high-throughput annotation and interpretation of genetic variants in cancer. It integrates information from various existing databases to convert DNA changes to protein changes, predict the functional effects of protein mutations, and provide insights into mutation frequencies, gene functions, variant effects, and clinical actionability. \n\nThe project aims to facilitate researchers and clinicians in understanding the implications of genetic variants, thereby enhancing the capabilities for genomic analysis in cancer research. Key functionalities include the ability to run the Genome Nexus application locally or in Docker containers, access to documentation for setup and usage, and support for local variant effect predictions using the Variant Effect Predictor (VEP). Additionally, it offers various deployment options to suit different user environments.'
highLevelGoals: Cancer Researchers aim to efficiently access and interpret genetic variant data to enhance their research outcomes in cancer studies.
Clinicians seek to utilize genetic data interpretations to make informed decisions regarding patient treatment and care plans.
Bioinformaticians require tools that simplify the analysis and interpretation of complex genetic data to derive meaningful insights.
Software Developers aim to have a flexible and robust application that can be easily deployed and enhanced to meet user needs.
Data Scientists seek to extract valuable insights from genomic data to support cancer research and inform clinical practices.
Administrators require effective tools and processes to manage the deployment and infrastructure of the Genome Nexus application.
Patients desire clear communication regarding the implications of genetic research and variant interpretations that may affect their health.
Curators aim to ensure the integrity and accuracy of the integrated databases to support reliable genetic variant interpretations.
The Genome Nexus project aims to consolidate genetic variant data from multiple sources to provide a unified resource for analysis and interpretation.
The Genome Nexus project seeks to deliver comprehensive insights that inform research and clinical decisions regarding genetic variants.
lowLevelGoals: Cancer Researchers can call an API to retrieve detailed information about a specific genetic variant using its unique identifier, including its clinical significance and associated studies.
Clinicians can call an API to submit their interpretation of a genetic variant for a specific patient case, including detailed treatment recommendations and rationale.
Bioinformaticians can call an API to apply specific analysis algorithms, such as variant calling or gene expression analysis, to a genetic data set to derive meaningful insights.

Software Developers can use an API call to deploy the Genome Nexus application to a designated cloud environment, specifying necessary configuration parameters such as resource allocation and environment settings.

Data Scientists can call an API to execute predefined queries on genomic data, extracting actionable insights relevant to cancer research, such as mutation prevalence and associated clinical outcomes.
Administrators can call an API to retrieve current performance metrics of the Genome Nexus application, including response times and error rates, to ensure it is functioning reliably.
Patients can call an API to request specific educational resources that explain genetic research and its implications for their health, tailored to their specific conditions.
Curators can call an API to submit new genetic variant information, including details about the variant, its clinical significance, and supporting evidence, to update the database.
The system can call an external genetic database API to retrieve and integrate genetic variant data into the Genome Nexus project, ensuring data consistency and integrity.
Users can call an API to obtain insights regarding mutation frequencies, gene functions, and clinical actionability of specific genetic variants, aiding in research and clinical decisions.
